definitions.definition: IPRs refer to the rights given to people over their creations. These creations can be literary, artistic, musical, or even inventions. IPRs protect the rights of creators over their work and give them exclusive rights to use, reproduce, and distribute their work.
A patent is a type of IPR that grants exclusive rights to an inventor over an invention for a specific period. This period is usually 20 years from the date of filing the patent application.
A trademark is a symbol, design, or expression that identifies a product or service of a particular company. It is used to distinguish a product or service from others in the market.

A copyright is a type of IPR that grants exclusive rights to the creators of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works. It protects the creators' rights over their work, including the right to reproduce, distribute, and display their work.
A software patent is a type of patent that protects software innovations. It grants exclusive rights to the creators of software over their inventions, including algorithms, methods, and processes.

A trade secret is a type of IPR that protects confidential business information, such as recipes, formulas, and manufacturing processes. It is not registered with any government authority and is protected through non-disclosure agreements and other means.

An industrial design is a type of IPR that protects the visual appearance of a product, including its shape, configuration, and ornamentation. It is used to distinguish a product from others in the market.

A GI is a type of IPR that protects the name of a region, place, or country that is associated with a product. It is used to distinguish a product from others in the market and to promote the product.
The right to reproduce refers to the exclusive right of a creator to reproduce their work, including making copies of the work.
The right to distribute refers to the exclusive right of a creator to distribute their work, including selling, assigning, or transferring the work to others.
The right to display refers to the exclusive right of a creator to display their work, including public display of the work.
The right to adapt refers to the exclusive right of a creator to adapt their work, including translating, transforming, or modifying the work.
Exclusive rights refer to the rights granted to a creator over their work, including the right to reproduce, distribute, and display the work, without the permission of others.
A derivative work is a work that is based on or derived from another work, including adaptations, translations, or modifications.
Plagiarism refers to the act of passing off someone else's work as one's own, without proper credit or permission.
Copyright infringement refers to the act of violating the exclusive rights of a creator over their work, including reproducing, distributing, or displaying the work without permission.
